trajectory_summary: The call produced a warm, self-booking customer who drove the final logistics himself — a genuine positive outcome. But the appointment is structurally fragile: the wife (who initiated the inquiry, holds the bill, and handles the finances) is not on the calendar, the bill was not collected, and the format was left ambiguous. Parker built real rapport but let the structural work slide once the conversation turned personal.
outcome_dispute: The appointment was booked, but its quality is conditional. The wife is the financial decision-maker by Quincy's own admission, and she is not included. Without the bill and without the wife, the closer is walking into a presentation to the wrong person with incomplete data. This should be tagged as a conditional booking, not a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: My wife handles all that shit, man. I I I got a couple of businesses. I keep my head up.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The wife initiated the inquiry, holds the bill, and manages the finances by Quincy's own explicit statement. She is not on the appointment. The closer will be presenting to the wrong person. This is the single highest-risk structural gap in the call — it directly threatens the sit quality and the setter's paycheck. The rep needs to hear that this line is a protocol trigger, not a conversational aside, and practice the pivot: 'Then we definitely want her on the call — she'll have the context to make this make sense. What time works for both of you?'

key_moments.quote: I think I had the 770 In Butterfield Road. Was that still the right address for you guys?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker opens with a records-based address confirmation rather than asking Quincy to provide it cold. This is a small but effective trust signal — it communicates that Ion already has Quincy's information and this is a legitimate follow-up, not a cold pitch. Quincy's immediate 'Yes. Right.' confirms the move landed.

key_moments.quote: My wife handles all that shit, man. I I I got a couple of businesses. I keep my head up.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the clearest decision-maker signal in the call. Quincy is not just mentioning his wife — he is explicitly naming her as the household's financial manager. Parker's response ('Gotcha. No. I totally get that.') treats this as a conversational aside rather than a protocol trigger. The correct move per the codex is to pivot immediately to spouse inclusion: 'Then we definitely want her on the call Friday — she'll have the context to make this make sense.' Parker does not make this move at any point in the call.

Quincy says he has no idea what the bill is. Parker drops the anchor entirely. The codex requires capture AND emotional flip — but even the capture attempt ends here. Parker had options: ask for a rough estimate ('ballpark — over $100? Over $200?'), bridge to the wife ('that's another reason to have her on the call — she'll know'), or use the unknown as curiosity bait ('that's actually one of the first things our specialist will pull up — most people in your area are surprised'). None of these happen.

key_moments.quote: I have two businesses. She has one, all retail. We have four kids. They're all in sports. I coach football, baseball, basketball. It's like, we just never literally get a free minute in it.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a load-profile signal, not just a scheduling complaint. Three businesses, four kids in sports, a household that 'never gets a free minute' — this is a high-consumption household. Parker hears it as a scheduling challenge and pivots to small talk. The bill_anchoring philosophy's capture-and-flip requirement applies here: the packed household description is evidence of energy consumption and bill pain potential. A rep who hears this should be thinking 'this household's bill is probably significant' and using that as a bridge, not a conversation topic.
